steps.expected: HTTP 200 with array containing one numeric price and one null price
steps.observed: HTTP 200. Array: [{price:1.0, cached:false}, {price:null, cached:false}]. Partial failure handled correctly.

evidence.notes: First element (USDC) has numeric price 1.0, second element (zero address) has price:null. Both have block_timestamp and cached fields. Response is 200 even with partial failure.

assertions.title: Batch with timestamp param works

steps.expected: HTTP 200 with both results sharing the same resolved block
steps.observed: HTTP 502 with error: 'Failed to resolve timestamp 1693067039 to block: Attribute BlockAtTimestamp.timestamp: expected type is datetime. Got: 1693067039'

evidence.containerLog: batch_timestamp_resolution_failed error="Attribute BlockAtTimestamp.timestamp: expected type is 'datetime'. Got: 1693067039"
evidence.notes: Bug: _resolve_block_from_timestamp passes int to get_block_at_timestamp but ypricemagic expects a datetime object. Same bug affects /price?timestamp= (seen in other validator logs). Retried once after 5s delay, same result.

assertions.issues: Timestamp resolution fails for batch endpoint. get_block_at_timestamp expects datetime, but server passes int. This is the same bug affecting the single /price?timestamp= endpoint.

assertions.title: Batch with timestamp and amounts combined

steps.expected: HTTP 200 with resolved block and amounts applied
steps.observed: HTTP 502 - same timestamp resolution bug as VAL-BATCH-010

evidence.notes: Blocked by same timestamp resolution bug as VAL-BATCH-010. Cannot test amounts+timestamp combination because timestamp resolution fails before amounts are processed.

assertions.issues: Same timestamp resolution bug as VAL-BATCH-010: get_block_at_timestamp expects datetime, server passes int.

steps.expected: HTTP 400 with error mentioning max token count
steps.observed: HTTP 400 with error: 'Too many tokens: 101. Maximum allowed is 100.'

evidence.notes: 101 addresses generated programmatically. Server correctly rejects with 400 and descriptive message.

steps.expected: HTTP 200 with block_timestamp field in every element

evidence.notes: Both elements contain block_timestamp field with Unix epoch integer 1693067039. First element was cache hit (from VAL-BATCH-009 batch), second was fresh fetch. Both have block_timestamp.

steps.expected: USDC priced and cached from batch
steps.observed: USDC returned with price:1.0 in batch response

steps.expected: cached:true from the batch-written cache entry

evidence.notes: After batch request at block 18000012, individual /price request for same token+block returns cached:true. Confirms batch writes individual cache entries.

assertions.title: Batch supports skip_cache and silent params

steps.expected: Params accepted and forwarded to get_prices internally
steps.observed: HTTP 200, both elements show cached:false despite cache entries existing (skip_cache observable). Params accepted without error.

evidence.notes: Observable: skip_cache=true causes cached:false even though cache entries exist from prior requests. silent=true is accepted without error. However, confirming kwargs are forwarded to get_prices() internally requires unit test inspection, not user-surface testing.

assertions.issues: Assertion requires 'unit test verifying kwargs' as evidence. Internal kwargs forwarding to get_prices() cannot be fully verified from user surface alone. Observable behavior (skip_cache bypasses cache, silent accepted) is consistent with correct forwarding, but not conclusive proof.

frictions.description: Token addresses in responses are redacted with asterisks (42-char strings), making it harder to verify token ordering in batch responses. Had to rely on position matching rather than address comparison.
frictions.resolution: Used input order position to verify correct mapping since the documentation mentions this is existing logger.py behavior.

blockers.description: Timestamp resolution is broken: _resolve_block_from_timestamp passes int to get_block_at_timestamp, but ypricemagic expects a datetime object. Error: 'Attribute BlockAtTimestamp.timestamp: expected type is datetime. Got: 1693067039'. This affects all timestamp-based batch requests.
blockers.quickFixAttempted: Retried VAL-BATCH-010 once after 5s delay. Same 502 error. Confirmed via docker compose logs that the error is consistent and deterministic (type mismatch, not transient).

summary: Tested 7 assertions: 4 passed (VAL-BATCH-009, VAL-BATCH-012, VAL-BATCH-013, VAL-BATCH-014), 2 failed (VAL-BATCH-010, VAL-BATCH-011 — timestamp resolution bug: server passes int to get_block_at_timestamp which expects datetime), 1 blocked (VAL-BATCH-015 — internal kwargs forwarding requires unit test, not verifiable from user surface).
